author: lightclient
content: Btw I did move signatures to the outer of the transaction as you suggested. I think it is the right thing to do. We also discussed making them a sidecar so they are totally separate. I think we are open to that idea still, but it the benefits felt a little too abstract at this stage. I also worked on a mechanism to reference pubkeys from state and wrote a brief spec about it in the rationale (if we have a key type to use it with, happy to promote to actual spec or make needed changes) which was inspired by your MAYO post: https://eips.sh/eip/8141#public-key-aliases

author: lightclient
content: would be happy to hear any further feedback you have

author: _shemnon
content: I think we should consider having second and later sigs also sign over the prior signatures (I think I saw that in EIP-8175 from <@429719902363058177> ) - the premise being latter sigantures are dependant on who signed the first signatures, like a paymaster over a specific payor.

author: lightclient
content: is this different than the transaction sender signing over all sigs in the transaction? my worry of allowing other types of "second sigs" is that the required mallebility can create annoying issues for the user including tx hash changing and at worst, maybe griefing?

author: _shemnon
content: Sig malliability is a big concern for multiple sigs. Having the sig in position one sign the tx, the sign in position two sign the tx and the first sig, etc prevents swapping sigs as the hash being signed changes per position.  Otherwise if we just added a "payor" sig to existing TXes with the same hash and the sig in position two is the paymaster, then we could just swap sig one and sig two, or duplicate sig two into sig one, and the TX does stuff it was never intended to do.  Multiple sigs does require sone way to lock in context, and signing over prior sigs privides that.  But it also prevents parallel construction of auth.  Arguably a good thing.
